headline: Waterloo, Vienna, and a Southern African warrior kingdom: the world after Napoleon
summary: 1815 closed the twenty-two-year cycle of revolutionary and Napoleonic war and opened a European century of comparative great-power peace. Napoleon returned from Elba on 1 March, ruled France for a Hundred Days, and was decisively beaten at Waterloo on 18 June by Wellington and Blucher; the Congress of Vienna signed its Final Act on 9 June and the Holy Alliance a few months later. In April Mount Tambora in the Dutch East Indies produced the largest volcanic eruption in recorded history, whose sulphate veil would give 1816 its Year Without a Summer. In southern Africa Shaka was consolidating the Zulu kingdom, in North America the Battle of New Orleans ended the War of 1812, and across Latin America Simon Bolivar and Jose de San Martin were laying plans for independence.

snapshots.text: Europe's diplomats had convened at Vienna in November 1814 under the Austrian chancellor Klemens von Metternich. Their negotiations, largely a Concert of the four great victors, Britain, Austria, Prussia and Russia, with Talleyrand's France skilfully readmitted, were interrupted on 7 March by news that Napoleon had escaped Elba. He landed at Golfe-Juan on 1 March, entered Paris on 20 March as Louis XVIII fled, and began the Hundred Days. The Congress finished its work all the same, signing the Final Act on 9 June: it redrew the map to create a Kingdom of the Netherlands, a German Confederation of thirty-nine states under Austrian presidency, an enlarged Prussia, a Congress Kingdom of Poland under the Russian tsar, and a restored papal state. On 18 June Wellington's Anglo-Allied army and Blucher's Prussians defeated Napoleon at Waterloo south of Brussels. Napoleon abdicated on 22 June, surrendered to HMS Bellerophon on 15 July, and was exiled to Saint Helena. On 26 September Alexander I of Russia, Francis I of Austria and Frederick William III of Prussia signed the Holy Alliance; the Quadruple Alliance followed on 20 November.

The Americas in 1815 were digesting the last of the Atlantic wars and stirring with the first of the wars of independence. The Treaty of Ghent, signed on 24 December 1814, had ended the War of 1812 between Britain and the United States, but news travelled slowly: on 8 January Andrew Jackson's mixed force of American regulars, Kentucky and Tennessee militia, Choctaw warriors, Baratarian privateers under Jean Lafitte and free black troops shattered a British assault on New Orleans in half an hour. President James Madison signed the treaty on 17 February, and the young republic emerged with a burst of nationalist confidence and the beginnings of what historians would call the Era of Good Feelings. In Spanish America, restored royalist rule under Fernando VII proved brittle: Simon Bolivar, forced to flee Cartagena, wrote his Letter from Jamaica in September, sketching an independent, republican Spanish America. Jose de San Martin was preparing the Army of the Andes at Mendoza. In Portuguese Brazil King Joao VI, exiled in Rio de Janeiro since 1808, elevated the colony to the status of a co-equal Kingdom of Brazil within a United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves on 16 December.

Qing China under the Jiaqing Emperor, seventeen years into his reign, was passing through a difficult middle period. The great White Lotus rebellion of 1796-1804 had drained the imperial treasury; the state was struggling to suppress piracy along the south China coast and coping with the first serious inflow of British Indian opium through the Canton trade. In 1815 the Company merchant William Milne joined Robert Morrison in Malacca to launch the first Chinese-language Protestant press, printing Morrison's translation of Christian scripture in defiance of the Qing edict against Christian preaching. In Vietnam the newly founded Nguyen dynasty of Emperor Gia Long, who had unified the country with French mercenary help in 1802, was completing the citadel at Hue on French Vauban lines. In Japan the Tokugawa shogunate under Ienari continued the long isolation of sakoku; Dutch and Chinese ships alone traded through Nagasaki. Korea remained Confucian and closed under King Sunjo of the Joseon dynasty.

In South Asia the East India Company had emerged from the Anglo-Nepalese War of 1814-16 in an ambiguous position: Major General David Ochterlony had driven Gurkha commanders including Amar Singh Thapa from Malaon and Jaithak by April 1815, but the war would drag on to the Treaty of Sugauli in March 1816. In the Punjab Ranjit Singh, thirty-five and already master of Lahore since 1799, was consolidating his Sikh empire and modernising his army with European mercenaries such as Jean-Francois Allard and Jean-Baptiste Ventura. The Maratha Confederacy, defeated by Wellesley in 1805, was preparing what would become the disastrous Third Anglo-Maratha War of 1817-18. Governor-General Lord Moira ruled in Calcutta. In central Asia the Emirate of Bukhara under Haydar Shah and the Khanate of Khiva under Muhammad Rahim Khan controlled the caravan routes between Persia and the Russian steppe frontier at Orenburg. The Qajar shah Fath Ali of Persia was still recovering from the loss of Georgia to Russia by the Treaty of Gulistan of 1813.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Mahmud II governed a still-vast but increasingly fissiparous realm. In Egypt the Albanian-born viceroy Muhammad Ali Pasha, having massacred the Mamluk beys in the Cairo citadel in 1811, was building a modern conscript army and monopolising cotton and grain exports; his son Ibrahim would soon be dispatched into Arabia to complete the destruction of the first Saudi state under the Wahhabi imam Abdullah bin Saud, culminating in the fall of Diriyah in 1818. On the Barbary coast the Second Barbary War of March-June 1815 saw an American squadron under Commodore Stephen Decatur force the dey of Algiers Omar Agha to sign a treaty ending tribute payments; a British-Dutch bombardment of Algiers would follow in 1816. In Serbia the Second Serbian Uprising, led by Milos Obrenovic, broke out on Palm Sunday, 23 April 1815; by autumn Obrenovic had negotiated a de facto autonomy from Istanbul. Persia's Qajar court in Tehran was hosting the British envoy Sir Gore Ouseley.

Southern Africa in 1815 was in the middle of a decade of political transformation later called the Mfecane, in which the small Zulu chiefdom north of the Tugela river was becoming a centralised military kingdom. Shaka, illegitimate son of the Zulu chief Senzangakhona and protege of the Mthethwa paramount Dingiswayo, had been leading his own regiment since 1810; in 1815 he was still Dingiswayo's vassal, but the tactical revolutions later associated with him, the short stabbing spear or iklwa, the amabutho regimental age-grade system, the buffalo-horn battle formation, were already reshaping the region. To the west the Sotho chief Moshoeshoe I was gathering refugees at Butha-Buthe. In West Africa the Sokoto Caliphate proclaimed by Usman dan Fodio in 1804 was consolidating across the Hausa states under his son Muhammad Bello. On the Gold Coast the Asante kingdom under Osei Bonsu dominated. British and American squadrons patrolled the coast under the 1807 slave-trade acts, though the Atlantic trade continued to grow to Cuba and Brazil. In Cape Town the recently confirmed British administration under Lord Charles Somerset was preparing to import 5,000 British settlers to the eastern frontier.

The event that most shaped 1815 in Oceania and, ultimately, the whole northern hemisphere, was the eruption of Mount Tambora on the island of Sumbawa in the Dutch East Indies. From 5 April, and climactically on 10-11 April, Tambora ejected an estimated 150 cubic kilometres of ash and pumice, killed perhaps 71,000 people directly on Sumbawa and neighbouring Lombok, and injected enough sulphate aerosol into the stratosphere to lower global temperatures by roughly one degree Celsius the following year, producing the 1816 Year Without a Summer in Europe and North America. In the fledgling colony of New South Wales, Governor Lachlan Macquarie's road across the Blue Mountains, completed under William Cox in January 1815, opened the interior to European pastoralism; the town of Bathurst was proclaimed on 7 May. In New Zealand the missionary Samuel Marsden's Church Missionary Society post at Rangihoua in the Bay of Islands, founded on Christmas Day 1814, welcomed the birth of the first European child in the country in February 1815. Across Polynesia King Kamehameha I ruled a unified Hawaiian kingdom in tacit dialogue with visiting American whalers.
